subject: State Farm Claim 4673B816T — Brian Dolbeare — No Hail Roof Denial

messages.body: Email to BDOLBEARE@yahoo.com, Subject: State Farm Claim 4673B816T. Attachment: 'No Hail RFG Denial 4673B816T Dolbeare.pdf' — formal state farm denial letter classifying the roof claim as no-hail.
This is the estimate for: 9129 Lakeland Valley Ct. Springfield, VA 22153. (Forwarded the State Farm denial PDF to Roof-ER marketing for contractor review).

denialText: State Farm denial classified as 'No Hail RFG Denial' for claim 4673B816T at 9129 Lakeland Valley Ct, Springfield VA 22153. Sent from statefarmfireclaims@statefarm.com on 2024-09-03. (Full denial language in PDF attachment 'No Hail RFG Denial 4673B816T Dolbeare.pdf'.)
carrierTactic: State Farm classifies the entire roof claim as 'No Hail' — the AI-driven binary determination from their patent US10565658 (Storm event roof damage detection) and US20220414781 (Probable Roof Loss Confidence Scores). When the AI's hail-confidence score doesn't cross threshold, the claim is automatically routed to a generic no-hail denial template.
patentMapping: State Farm patents US10565658 (Storm event roof damage detection) + US20220414781 (Probable Roof Loss Confidence Scores) drive this binary classification. The 'No Hail RFG Denial' template suggests AI confidence below threshold — the homeowner can rebut by showing patent-mapped evidence (creasing on surrounding shingles, soft metal damage, neighbor approvals).
lessonForAnalyzer: When a State Farm denial uses 'No Hail' framing, the counter-letter should: (1) cite specific hail evidence the AI scanner likely missed (test square hits per pattern in carrier-specific patent), (2) request the underlying confidence score under emerging AI disclosure rules, (3) reference neighbor approvals if available (forces adjuster to explain why same storm = different outcome).
